subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Clear section headings with introduction, body and conclusion structure
Figures and tables are numbered consistently
weaknesses: Lengthy digressions (full questionnaire, raw data) interrupt narrative
Unnecessary detailed derivations and descriptive paragraphs
Layout cohesion issues (e.g. split subtitle line)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments show that while the exploration has some coherence and logical development, lengthy digressions and embedded raw material disrupt the flow (band 2). Organization is adequate with clear headings (band 3), but conciseness is hampered by unnecessary elements (band 2). Overall, a holistic score of 2 reflects âsome coherence and logical developmentâ with room to streamline content.

strengths: Introduction of key statistical terms (mean, SD, H statistic, p-value) with notation
Effective use of a variety of graphical and tabulated representations
Overall support of the argument with multiple plots and tests
weaknesses: Inconsistent formatting of variables and Greek letters
Misprints and compressed or truncated labels reduce clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work uses some appropriate mathematical language and notation but with inconsistencies and undefined symbols (band 2). Multiple representations are effective (band 3), yet occasional misprints and inconsistencies in communication mean the clarity is only mostly consistent (band 2). A holistic score of 2 balances these strengths and weaknesses.

strengths: Topic selection clearly driven by personal interest in school commuting
Bespoke survey design and personal commentary show genuine engagement
weaknesses: Limited creativity beyond standard statistical procedures
No novel modelling or original mathematical construction
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: There is clear personal engagement and independent thinking in choosing the topic and survey design (band 2), but creativity and initiative are limited to minor extensions (band 1). Overall, the personal approach drives the exploration but does not demonstrate significant originality, warranting a score of 1.

strengths: Identification of key limitations (route distance, sample size imbalance, licence age)
Meaningful suggestions for larger samples and methodological improvements
weaknesses: Critical analysis of statistical choices is minimal
Connections to enhanced understanding are brief
Reflection lacks depth on practical implications
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides some meaningful reflection on validity throughout (band 2), but critical analysis remains basic (band 1) and links to deeper mathematical understanding are limited (band 1). A holistic score of 1 best represents this level of reflection.

strengths: Use of relevant SL-level mathematics (descriptive stats, Shapiro-Wilk, Kruskal-Wallis)
Procedures appropriately applied to both research questions
Demonstrates sound understanding of non-parametric testing rationale
weaknesses: Minor errors in formulae (rounding slips, denominator misuse)
Unclear notation in real speed and Shapiro-Wilk formulas
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematics is consistently at course level and mostly correct (band 3), with sound but not sophisticated understanding (band 3). Procedures are relevant and conclusions follow correctly, placing the work solidly in band 3 for Use of Mathematics, meriting a score of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Selecting a personally relevant theme (school commuting) and designing a bespoke survey demonstrates meaningful student choice.  The decision to compute a âreal speedâ measure and to carry out non-parametric testing also indicates thinking beyond textbook examples, qualifying as âsignificant evidence of independent thinkingââband 2.

Explanations of statistical concepts indicate sound, if not deep, understanding (e.g. rationale for non-parametric test, definition of degrees of freedom).  Occasional misconceptions (claim that SD is âsum of the measureâ) prevent a higher band, but âgood understanding in partsâ (band 3) is demonstrated.

Sample calculations are demonstrated and spreadsheet output supports results.  Minor slips are present (rounding, mis-typed denominator in H formula) but conclusions (e.g. rejecting H0 in KW test) are consistent with computed values.  Therefore accuracy is âmostly correctâ, aligning with band 3.

Considerable material (full questionnaire, full raw data, repeated tables) is embedded in the main body, making the exploration over-long (â30 pages).  Some sentences digress into general transport advocacy, and several formula derivations are unnecessarily detailed for the stated purpose.  Despite this, most content is relevant to the investigation, so âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ (band 2) is most fitting.

Key statistical terms (mean, SD, quartiles, H statistic, p-value) are introduced, and notation such as H, W and Î± is used.  Nevertheless, variables are inconsistently formatted (mix of italics, subscripts, stray Greek letters unrelated to context) and several symbols (Ï_max, Ï_max) have no definition.  This intermittent correctness aligns with âsome appropriate mathematical language and notationâ (band 2).

Explanations are usually understandable but lapses occur (e.g. mis-typed subscripts, missing words, duplicated tables).  The reader can reconstruct calculations, yet must work around inconsistencies, so communication is âmostly clear with some consistencyââband 2.

The mathematics (descriptive statistics, Shapiro-Wilk test, Kruskal-Wallis test, basic speed calculation) is all within the AI SL syllabus and directly addresses both research questions, showing relevance.  Procedures are appropriate but not sophisticated, so band 3 (relevant mathematics at course level used) is appropriate.

The student employs box-and-whisker plots, a pie chart, a bar chart with error bars, a scatter plot and tabulated statistics.  These representations are generally appropriate and assist the reader in following the argument, though labelling is occasionally compressed or truncated.  The variety and effectiveness match the âeffective use of multiple representationsâ descriptor (band 3).

The evaluation section lists several limitations (straight-line distance vs actual route, unequal sample sizes, licence age).  These comments show the student has reflected on validity throughout, not merely at the end.  The reflection is meaningful but not deeply critical, fitting band 2.

Beyond selecting statistical techniques, the student largely follows standard procedures; no novel modelling or original mathematical construction is attempted.  Minor creative touches (defining âreal speedâ) provide âsome creativity or initiativeâ, consistent with band 1.

The report follows the classical IA structure (introductionâmethodâresultsâevaluation) and ideas usually flow in the order in which they are needed.  However, lengthy digressions (e.g. several pages of raw questionnaire text, two-page Shapiro-Wilk interpolation explanation) break the narrative and the statistical argument is occasionally interrupted by descriptive paragraphs.  Overall, the logical line is visible but not always smooth, so âsomeâ coherence and development is best captured by band 2.

Critical analysis is limited to acknowledging constraints and suggesting larger samples; there is little probing of statistical decisions (e.g. effect of excluding taxi group, possible regression diagnostics).  This basic critique sits in band 1.

The student occasionally links findings to âurban transport designâ and personal commuting choices, but these connections are brief and not shown to enhance mathematical understanding.  Thus only âlimited connection to understandingâ is evidenced â band 1.

Each major section is clearly headed; appendices are referenced; figures and tables are numbered.  Introduction leads into aims/hypotheses and a conclusion/evaluation closes the work, demonstrating a recognisable âintroductionâbodyâconclusionâ organisation.  Transitions are not always seamless and repetition occurs, so this matches the âwell organisedâ description of band 3 rather than the exemplary flow of band 4.

The exploration is clearly driven by the studentâs curiosity about transport efficiency and sleep.  Personal commentary (love of maps, advocacy for public transit) shapes the investigation and choice of variables, illustrating a âclear personal approachâ (band 2).

annotations.comment: The title page is clear and appropriately labeled but could be more concise by removing the split line for the subtitle to improve layout cohesion.
The introductory definition of real speed states it as the relation between time and distance but would be clearer if you explicitly note it is distance divided by time.
The personal commentary on urban maps demonstrates clear personal engagement and shows enthusiasm for the topic.
Consider adding a statement regarding ethical approval or consent for the questionnaire to strengthen the Method section.
The pie chart in Figure 1 lacks clear percentage labels on each slice, making interpretation of proportions difficult.
The mean formula includes an undefined symbol Ïâââ; define all symbols or remove extraneous notation to maintain clarity.
The sample standard deviation formula should divide by nâ1 rather than n to reflect sample rather than population SD.
Notation Ïâââ and Ïâff in the median and IQR definitions are undefined; replace with standard symbols or remove.
The distance and time box plots are effective but would benefit from including sample sizes and units on axes for consistency.
The stray expression âÎâ¼10â»Â¹Â¹â in the list of demographic questions is irrelevant and appears to be an OCR or copy error; please remove or clarify.
The real speed formula $$[ 	ext{km/h} ] =  rac{	ext{distance [km]}}{	ext{time [min]}  div 60}$$ is unclear; rewrite as $$v =  rac{d}{t/60}$$.
The bar graph of mean real speeds (Figure 5) lacks error bars or sample size annotations, which limits interpretation of variability.
The ShapiroâWilk test formula does not define coefficients \(a_i\); add explanation that these come from Shapiro & Wilk (1965).
The interpolation formula should specify which Wâ,pâ and Wâ,pâ values are used from the ShapiroâWilk table.
The KruskalâWallis formula uses \(\bar{r}_{ij}\) incorrectly; it should refer to individual ranks $$r_{ij}$$ rather than group mean.
The inline text âWâverwaltungâ = 0.874â appears to be a misprint (âwalkingâ intended); correct the variable name for consistency.
In Table 1, include the chosen significance level $$\alpha=0.05$$ for every row to clarify comparisons.
Ensure the denominator 20807 used in the H calculation is justified, perhaps by describing spreadsheet DEVSQ workings.
The scatter plot of sleep time versus real speed (Figure 7) would be enhanced by adding a regression line and reporting the correlation coefficient.
The suggestion to use DunnâBonferroni tests in Extensions successfully demonstrates initiative for further analysis.
References are formatted consistently but URLs could be presented in hanging indent style to improve readability of long links.
Overall use of multiple plots, tables, and tests shows effective use of multiple representations to support the investigation.
The conclusion restates results but could be more reflective by discussing the implications of speed differences in practical terms.
The evaluation acknowledges limitations but lacks critical analysis of how nonânormal groups impact the validity of KruskalâWallis results.
